patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] mm/swap: fix swap cache memcg accounting

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm/swap: fix swap cache memcg accounting

patches_submitted.patch_summary: The patch fixes a swap cache memcg accounting issue by charging the folio in the memcg before adding it to the swap cache, ensuring correct accounting at the node level.

review_comments.author: Kairui Song
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged the fix as clean and good enough for now, but suggested moving lruvec static info out of __swap_cache_add_folio in a future optimization.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, Mar 20, 2026 at 06:05:59AM +0800, Alexandre Ghiti wrote:
> The swap readahead path was recently refactored and while doing this,
> the order between the charging of the folio in the memcg and the addition
> of the folio in the swap cache was inverted.
> 
> Since the accounting of the folio is done while adding the folio to the
> swap cache and the folio is not charged in the memcg yet, the accounting
> is then done at the node level, which is wrong.
> 
> Fix this by charging the folio in the memcg before adding it to the swap cache.
> 
> Fixes: 2732acda82c9 ("mm, swap: use swap cache as the swap in synchronize layer")
> Signed-off-by: Alexandre Ghiti <alex@ghiti.fr>
> ---
>  mm/swap_state.c | 9 ++++-----
>  1 file changed, 4 insertions(+), 5 deletions(-)
> 
> diff --git a/mm/swap_state.c b/mm/swap_state.c
> index 6d0eef7470be..48aff2c917c0 100644
> --- a/mm/swap_state.c
> +++ b/mm/swap_state.c
> @@ -494,6 +494,10 @@ static struct folio *__swap_cache_prepare_and_add(swp_entry_t entry,
>  
>  	__folio_set_locked(folio);
>  	__folio_set_swapbacked(folio);
> +
> +	if (!charged && mem_cgroup_swapin_charge_folio(folio, NULL, gfp, entry))
> +		goto failed;
> +

Thanks! I was worrying charging it first could cause thrashing, raced
swapin will cause multiple charge and failed one will uncharge, so
I put the charge after insert into cache, completely forgot about
the lruvec static info part.

For a quick fix this this is clean and good enough, and it
doesn't effect the most performance sensitive part, mTHP and
ZRAM would have `charged == true` here so they are not effected.

Later we can move the lruvec static info out of
__swap_cache_add_folio after the charge, maybe I'll do this with
someother follow up optimzation together, then everything should
be perfect.

And I think we need Cc stable here.

Acked-by: Kairui Song <kasong@tencent.com>

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged the patch as correct.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, Mar 20, 2026 at 06:05:59AM +0100, Alexandre Ghiti wrote:
> The swap readahead path was recently refactored and while doing this,
> the order between the charging of the folio in the memcg and the addition
> of the folio in the swap cache was inverted.
> 
> Since the accounting of the folio is done while adding the folio to the
> swap cache and the folio is not charged in the memcg yet, the accounting
> is then done at the node level, which is wrong.
> 
> Fix this by charging the folio in the memcg before adding it to the swap cache.
> 
> Fixes: 2732acda82c9 ("mm, swap: use swap cache as the swap in synchronize layer")
> Signed-off-by: Alexandre Ghiti <alex@ghiti.fr>

Acked-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham
review_comments.summary: Reviewed the patch and found it to be correct.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, Mar 19, 2026 at 10:06 PM Alexandre Ghiti <alex@ghiti.fr> wrote:
>
> The swap readahead path was recently refactored and while doing this,
> the order between the charging of the folio in the memcg and the addition
> of the folio in the swap cache was inverted.
>
> Since the accounting of the folio is done while adding the folio to the
> swap cache and the folio is not charged in the memcg yet, the accounting
> is then done at the node level, which is wrong.
>
> Fix this by charging the folio in the memcg before adding it to the swap cache.
>
> Fixes: 2732acda82c9 ("mm, swap: use swap cache as the swap in synchronize layer")
> Signed-off-by: Alexandre Ghiti <alex@ghiti.fr>
> ---
>  mm/swap_state.c | 9 ++++-----
>  1 file changed, 4 insertions(+), 5 deletions(-)
>
> diff --git a/mm/swap_state.c b/mm/swap_state.c
> index 6d0eef7470be..48aff2c917c0 100644
> --- a/mm/swap_state.c
> +++ b/mm/swap_state.c
> @@ -494,6 +494,10 @@ static struct folio *__swap_cache_prepare_and_add(swp_entry_t entry,
>
>         __folio_set_locked(folio);
>         __folio_set_swapbacked(folio);
> +
> +       if (!charged && mem_cgroup_swapin_charge_folio(folio, NULL, gfp, entry))
> +               goto failed;
> +
>         for (;;) {
>                 ret = swap_cache_add_folio(folio, entry, &shadow);
>                 if (!ret)
> @@ -514,11 +518,6 @@ static struct folio *__swap_cache_prepare_and_add(swp_entry_t entry,
>                         goto failed;
>         }
>

Reviewed-by: Nhat Pham <nphamcs@gmail.com>

review_comments.author: Chris Li
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged the patch as correct.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, Mar 19, 2026 at 10:06 PM Alexandre Ghiti <alex@ghiti.fr> wrote:
>
> The swap readahead path was recently refactored and while doing this,
> the order between the charging of the folio in the memcg and the addition
> of the folio in the swap cache was inverted.
>
> Since the accounting of the folio is done while adding the folio to the
> swap cache and the folio is not charged in the memcg yet, the accounting
> is then done at the node level, which is wrong.
>
> Fix this by charging the folio in the memcg before adding it to the swap cache.
>
> Fixes: 2732acda82c9 ("mm, swap: use swap cache as the swap in synchronize layer")
> Signed-off-by: Alexandre Ghiti <alex@ghiti.fr>

Acked-by: Chris Li <chrisl@kernel.org>

Chris

> ---
>  mm/swap_state.c | 9 ++++-----
>  1 file changed, 4 insertions(+), 5 deletions(-)
>
> diff --git a/mm/swap_state.c b/mm/swap_state.c
> index 6d0eef7470be..48aff2c917c0 100644
> --- a/mm/swap_state.c
> +++ b/mm/swap_state.c
> @@ -494,6 +494,10 @@ static struct folio *__swap_cache_prepare_and_add(swp_entry_t entry,
>
>         __folio_set_locked(folio);
>         __folio_set_swapbacked(folio);
> +
> +       if (!charged && mem_cgroup_swapin_charge_folio(folio, NULL, gfp, entry))
> +               goto failed;
> +
>         for (;;) {
>                 ret = swap_cache_add_folio(folio, entry, &shadow);
>                 if (!ret)
> @@ -514,11 +518,6 @@ static struct folio *__swap_cache_prepare_and_add(swp_entry_t entry,
>                         goto failed;
>         }
>
> -       if (!charged && mem_cgroup_swapin_charge_folio(folio, NULL, gfp, entry)) {
> -               swap_cache_del_folio(folio);
> -               goto failed;
> -       }
> -
>         memcg1_swapin(entry, folio_nr_pages(folio));
>         if (shadow)
>                 workingset_refault(folio, shadow);
> --
> 2.53.0
>
>

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 2/4] locking/percpu-rwsem: Extract __percpu_up_read()
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch extracts the slowpath of percpu_up_read() into a new function __percpu_up_read(). The original percpu_up_read() is simplified to call this new function when necessary, reducing binary size by avoiding inlining. A tracepoint can now be added to the __percpu_up_read() function without increasing the binary size. This change improves maintainability and flexibility for future development.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 3/4] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to sleepable locks

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 3/4] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to sleepable locks

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new tracepoint called contended_release to the Linux kernel's locking subsystem. The contended_release tracepoint fires on the holder side when a contended lock is released, allowing for correlation of lock hold time under contention with waiter events by lock address. The patch modifies various slowpath unlock paths in sleepable locks (mutex, rtmutex, semaphore, rwsem, percpu-rwsem, and RT-specific rwbase locks) to fire the tracepoint before or after the lock is released, depending on the specific lock type. This enables more detailed tracing of locking behavior.

patches_submitted.subject: [RFC PATCH v3 4/4] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to spinning locks

version_history.subject: [RFC PATCH v3 4/4] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to spinning locks

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a contended_release tracepoint to spinning locks, specifically queued spinlocks and rwlocks. The tracepoint is designed to minimize the impact on the hot path when enabled, using an out-of-line function that combines contention check, trace call, and unlock operations. When disabled, only a single NOP instruction is added to the hot path. The patch also includes changes to handle inlined unlock cases, which could not be achieved through Kconfig options alone.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 1/4] tracing/lock: Remove unnecessary linux/sched.h include

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 1/4] tracing/lock: Remove unnecessary linux/sched.h include

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch removes an unnecessary include statement from the lock tracing header file, as none of the trace events in this file reference anything from linux/sched.h.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 0/4] locking: contended_release tracepoint instrumentation
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series introduces a new tracepoint called contended_release, which fires on the lock holder's side when a lock is released under contention. The goal is to provide more accurate hold time estimation and capture the holder's stack at release time. The series includes instrumentation for various sleepable locks, including mutexes, rtmutexes, semaphores, rwsems, percpu-rwsems, and rwbase_rts.

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: AI review link provided, indicating that the reviewer is waiting for more feedback before providing a detailed review.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, 18 Mar 2026 18:45:17 +0000 Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:

> The existing contention_begin/contention_end tracepoints fire on the
> waiter side. The lock holder's identity and stack can be captured at
> contention_begin time (e.g. perf lock contention --lock-owner), but
> this reflects the holder's state when a waiter arrives, not when the
> lock is actually released.
> 
> This series adds a contended_release tracepoint that fires on the
> holder side when a lock with waiters is released. This provides:
> 
> - Hold time estimation: when the holder's own acquisition was
>   contended, its contention_end (acquisition) and contended_release
>   can be correlated to measure how long the lock was held under
>   contention.
> 
> - The holder's stack at release time, which may differ from what perf lock
>   contention --lock-owner captures if the holder does significant work between
>   the waiter's arrival and the unlock.
> 
> The series is structured as follows:
> 
> 1. Remove unnecessary linux/sched.h include from trace/events/lock.h.
> 2. Extract __percpu_up_read() out of the inline percpu_up_read() to
>    avoid binary size increase from adding a tracepoint.
> 3. Add contended_release tracepoint and instrument sleepable locks:
>    mutex, rtmutex, semaphore, rwsem, percpu-rwsem, and rwbase_rt.

AI review:
	https://sashiko.dev/#/patchset/cover.1773858853.git.d@ilvokhin.com

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/8] dax/kmem: atomic whole-device hotplug via sysfs

version_history.subject: [PATCH 0/8] dax/kmem: atomic whole-device hotplug via sysfs

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/8] mm/memory-tiers: consolidate memory type dedup into mt_get_memory_type()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch consolidates memory type deduplication into a single function, mt_get_memory_type(), which checks against the global default_memory_types list under memory_tier_lock. The per-driver lists and associated infrastructure are removed as they only provided deduplication within a single driver. A new constant, MEMTIER_DEFAULT_LOWTIER_ADISTANCE, is introduced to replace the default DAX adistance, making it clearer where other drivers should place their memory in relation to the default lower tier.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/8] mm/memory: add memory_block_align_range() helper
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new helper function called `memory_block_align_range()` to the Linux kernel's memory management code. The function takes an input range and aligns its start address up and end address down to memory block boundaries, which is required for memory hotplug operations. This helper function is added as a common utility in the `<linux/memory.h>` header file and is used by the `dax/kmem` driver to ensure that memory ranges are properly aligned during hotplug operations.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/8] mm/memory_hotplug: pass online_type to online_memory_block() via arg
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the `online_memory_block` function to accept the online type as an argument, rather than using a default value. This change prepares for future patches that will allow callers to specify explicit online types. The caller in `add_memory_resource` is updated to pass the default online type via a local variable. No functional changes are made.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 4/8] mm/memory_hotplug: export mhp_get_default_online_type
series_items.patch_summary: This patch exports the mhp_get_default_online_type function from the mm/memory_hotplug module, allowing drivers to use it when hotplug is disabled. The function returns MMOP_OFFLINE by default as it's non-destructive and can't return -1. This change enables drivers like cxl that co-mingle hotplug and DAX use-cases to build even when hotplug is disabled.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 5/8] mm/memory_hotplug: add __add_memory_driver_managed() with online_type arg
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the memory hotplug API to add a new interface that allows drivers to select their preferred online type (ZONE_NORMAL vs ZONE_MOVABLE) when adding memory. The CXL driver, which uses the dax kmem driver, requires this feature. A new helper function __add_memory_driver_managed is added, which accepts an explicit online_type argument and validates it. Existing APIs are modified to use the new helpers, but no functional changes are made for existing users.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 6/8] dax: plumb hotplug online_type through dax
series_items.patch_summary: This patch allows drivers that use the dax_kmem driver to specify an auto-online policy when creating a DAX device. The policy is specified through the 'online_type' field, which can be set to a preferred auto-online policy or left to default to the system's default policy. This change enables more flexibility and control over how memory is online-ed for drivers that use dax_kmem. No functional changes are made to existing callers, ensuring backward compatibility.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 7/8] dax/kmem: extract hotplug/hotremove helper functions
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the kmem probe and remove functions by extracting initialization, cleanup, hotplug, and hot-remove logic into separate helper functions. The new helpers enable future extensions to support more granular control over memory hotplug operations. Specifically, it introduces four new functions: dax_kmem_init_resources, dax_kmem_cleanup_resources, dax_kmem_do_hotplug, and dax_kmem_do_hotremove. These functions handle tasks such as initializing resources, cleaning up initialized resources, handling memory region reservation and addition, and handling memory removal and resource cleanup. The patch is a pure refactoring with no functional change, making it easier to extend the code in the future.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 8/8] dax/kmem: add sysfs interface for atomic whole-device hotplug
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a sysfs interface to control and query the state of entire memory regions in the dax kmem driver. The new 'hotplug' attribute allows userspace to set the state to 'unplugged', 'online', or 'online_movable'. The initial state after probe is modified to correctly handle the creation of memory blocks. Additionally, the patch addresses a race condition by lifting the removal of memory blocks during unbind into a WARN and preventing deadlock.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series introduces atomic whole-device hotplug control via sysfs, addressing issues with the current non-atomic interface for dax hotplug. It adds a 'hotplug' attribute to support three states: 'unplug', 'online', and 'online_movable'. The patches also include cleanups and refactoring of existing code to prepare the mm and dax layers for this feature.

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Raised the issue of userland tools interfering with atomic hotplug, pointing out that AI review has more comments on this topic. Did not provide specific technical feedback or opinions.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Sat, 21 Mar 2026 11:03:56 -0400 Gregory Price <gourry@gourry.net> wrote:

> The dax kmem driver currently onlines memory during probe using the
> system default policy, with no way to control or query the region state
> at runtime - other than by inspecting the state of individual blocks.
> 
> Offlining and removing an entire region requires operating on individual
> memory blocks, creating race conditions where external entities can
> interfere between the offline and remove steps.
> 
> The problem was discussed specifically in the LPC2025 device memory
> sessions - https://lpc.events/event/19/contributions/2016/ - where
> it was discussed how the non-atomic interface for dax hotplug is causing
> issues in some distributions which have competing userland controllers
> that interfere with each other.
> 
> This series adds a sysfs "hotplug" attribute for atomic whole-device
> hotplug control, along with the mm and dax plumbing to support it.

AI review (which hasn't completed at this time) has a lot to say:
	https://sashiko.dev/#/patchset/20260321150404.3288786-1-gourry@gourry.net

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged some issues with the patch, including a UAF and an extern left in one of the patches. Promised to address these in a respin and provided additional context on userland tools interfering with atomic hotplug.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Sat, Mar 21, 2026 at 10:40:21AM -0700, Andrew Morton wrote:
> On Sat, 21 Mar 2026 11:03:56 -0400 Gregory Price <gourry@gourry.net> wrote:
> 
> > The dax kmem driver currently onlines memory during probe using the
> > system default policy, with no way to control or query the region state
> > at runtime - other than by inspecting the state of individual blocks.
> > 
> > Offlining and removing an entire region requires operating on individual
> > memory blocks, creating race conditions where external entities can
> > interfere between the offline and remove steps.
> > 
> > The problem was discussed specifically in the LPC2025 device memory
> > sessions - https://lpc.events/event/19/contributions/2016/ - where
> > it was discussed how the non-atomic interface for dax hotplug is causing
> > issues in some distributions which have competing userland controllers
> > that interfere with each other.
> > 
> > This series adds a sysfs "hotplug" attribute for atomic whole-device
> > hotplug control, along with the mm and dax plumbing to support it.
> 
> AI review (which hasn't completed at this time) has a lot to say:
> 	https://sashiko.dev/#/patchset/20260321150404.3288786-1-gourry@gourry.net

Looking at the results - i mucked up a UAF during the rebase that i
didn't catch during testing.  Will clean that up.

I also just realized I left an extern in one of the patches that I
thought I had removed.

So I owe a respin on this in more ways than one.

But on the AI review comment for non-trivial stuff
---

Much of the remaining commentary is about either the pre-existing code
race conditions, or design questions in the space of that race
condition.

Specifically: userland can still try to twiddle the memoryN/state bits
while the dax device loops over non-contiguous regions.

I dropped this commit:
https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260114235022.3437787-6-gourry@gourry.net/

>From the series, because the feedback here:
https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/d1938a63-839b-44a5-a68f-34ad290fef21@kernel.org/

suggested that offline_and_remove_memory() would resolve the race
condition problem - but the patch proposed actually solved two issues:

1) Inconsistent hotplug state issue (user is still using the old
   per-block offlining pattern)

2) The old offline pattern calling BUG() instead of WARN() when trying
   to unbind while things are still online.

But this goes to the issue of:  If the race condition in userland has
been around for many years, is it to be considered a feature we should
not break - or on what time scale should we consider breaking it?

I don't know the answer, David will have to weigh in on that.

~Gregory

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] md/raid0: use kvzalloc/kvfree for strip_zone and devlist allocations

version_history.subject: [PATCH] md/raid0: use kvzalloc/kvfree for strip_zone and devlist allocations

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses a WARNING in the RAID0 driver caused by large allocation sizes exceeding MAX_PAGE_ORDER, leading to a WARN_ON_ONCE_GFP condition. The patch converts strip_zone and devlist allocations from kzalloc/kzalloc_objs to kvzalloc/kvfree, which first attempts contiguous allocation with __GFP_NOWARN and falls back to vmalloc for large sizes.

review_comments.author: Yu Kuai
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch with a Reviewed-by tag. The change is correct and should fix the issue.
review_comments.raw_body:
在 2026/3/9 7:42, Gregory Price 写道:

> syzbot reported a WARNING at mm/page_alloc.c:__alloc_frozen_pages_noprof()
> triggered by create_strip_zones() in the RAID0 driver.
>
> When raid_disks is large, the allocation size exceeds MAX_PAGE_ORDER (4MB
> on x86), causing WARN_ON_ONCE_GFP(order > MAX_PAGE_ORDER).
>
> Convert the strip_zone and devlist allocations from kzalloc/kzalloc_objs to
> kvzalloc/kvzalloc_objs, which first attempts a contiguous allocation with
> __GFP_NOWARN and then falls back to vmalloc for large sizes. Convert the
> corresponding kfree calls to kvfree.
>
> Both arrays are pure metadata lookup tables (arrays of pointers and zone
> descriptors) accessed only via indexing, so they do not require physically
> contiguous memory.
>
> Reported-by:syzbot+924649752adf0d3ac9dd@syzkaller.appspotmail.com
> Signed-off-by: Gregory Price<gourry@gourry.net>
> ---
>   drivers/md/raid0.c | 18 +++++++++---------
>   1 file changed, 9 insertions(+), 9 deletions(-)

Reviewed-by: Yu Kuai <yukuai@fnnas.com>

-- 
Thansk,
Kuai

review_comments.author: Li Nan
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch with a Reviewed-by tag. The change is correct and should fix the issue.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 0/8] dax/kmem: atomic whole-device hotplug via sysfs
Re: [PATCH v3 0/8] io_uring: add kernel-managed buffer rings

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: add BTRFS_IOC_GET_CSUMS ioctl
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new ioctl (input/output control) called BTRFS_IOC_GET_CSUMS, which allows userspace to query the on-disk checksums for a file without needing to read the data itself. The ioctl takes an argument specifying the offset and length of interest, as well as a buffer to store the results. The kernel returns a struct containing information about whether the checksum is available, and if so, what type it is (e.g., sparse, preallocated, or compressed). This feature is primarily intended for speeding up mkfs.btrfs --rootdir operations, particularly when using the --reflink option, which can result in significant performance improvements.

review_comments.author: Mark Harmstone (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the patch does not address mkfs.btrfs performance and instead pointed to a separate pull request for improvements.
review_comments.raw_body:
mkfs patches for this: https://github.com/kdave/btrfs-progs/pull/1096

On 20/03/2026 12.50 pm, Mark Harmstone wrote:

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Qu Wenruo expressed concerns about the handling of hole and preallocated ranges in the BTRFS_GET_CSUMS_SPARSE type, suggesting that they should be treated differently due to their distinct implications for mkfs behavior. Specifically, holes should not create any extent instead of writing zero, while preallocated ranges can indicate no csums to allow mkfs to distinguish between them and change to zero writes accordingly.

Reviewer Qu Wenruo suggested adding a special flag to indicate when csums are skipped for compressed extents, instead of using NO_CSUMS, to improve distinguishability between holes and compressed data.

Reviewer Qu Wenruo suggested combining btrfs_ioctl_get_csums_entry and real buffer for csum into one structure, and removing redundant offset/length fields from the entry structure.
review_comments.raw_body:
I'm not sure if it's a good idea to put hole and preallocated range into 
the same BTRFS_GET_CSUMS_SPARSE.

Although both means there is no csum, hole case means there is really no 
data extent, thus we should not create any extent instead of writing zero.

For preallocated, indicating it has no CSUM can allow mkfs to 
distinguish hole and preallocated, thus change to zero writes to 
prealloc, which is faster and make the resulted fs more aligned to the 
source dir.


And for EOF checks, I think we don't need to bother that much, aka, just 
let it return the regular results.

My assumption is, the mkfs shouldn't pass a range completely beyond the 
round_up(i_size), as non-reflink rootdir population would always read 
out the content of the inode from the host fs.
Thus we won't really read beyond the inode size.

---

I agree with the skip of compressed extents, but I'd prefer to have a 
special flag to indicate that, other than NO_CSUMS.

Or mkfs is unable to distinguish hole and compressed extents.

[...]

---

From the progs usage, it is always a single btrfs_ioctl_get_csums_entry 
at the beginning of buf[], then real buffer for csum, can we just 
combine both structures into one?

Furthermore, since we only query one extent at one time, the 
offset/length are more or less duplicated between args and entry structure.

We can just save the length into the args without the need for entry 
members (except the type).

Thanks,
Qu

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 21/21] vswap: batch contiguous vswap free calls

version_history.subject: [PATCH v4 21/21] vswap: batch contiguous vswap free calls
[PATCH v5 21/21] vswap: batch contiguous vswap free calls

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 00/21] Virtual Swap Space
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series introduces virtual swap space, a new approach to managing swap memory. It addresses lock contention issues on the virtual swap layer and reduces memory overhead by optimizing the swap descriptor size. The patches also fix various regressions reported by kernel developers and users, including deadlocks and poor swap free batching behavior.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the swap cache needs to be decoupled from physical swap infrastructure and will manage it at the virtual swap layer. They plan to remove swap cache related helpers of swap table, repurpose the rest of the swap table as rmap (physical -> virtual swap mapping), and temporarily use a single global lock for swap cache accesses.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the organization of the swap API in include/linux/swap.h by grouping functions into categories and removing extern keywords, stating that this is purely a clean-up with no functional changes intended.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham (author)
review_comments.summary: The author acknowledged that the swapoff path needs to drop the per-vswap spinlock before calling try_to_unmap(), agreed to restructure in v2, and provided a generic API abstraction for this behavior.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author addressed a concern about the lack of abstraction in zswap entry operations by adding new helper functions to facilitate re-implementation when swap is virtualized.

Author addressed a concern about the swap_zswap_tree() function being called without checking if it's empty, acknowledged that this is an oversight and agreed to replace these calls with zswap_entry_load() or zswap_entry_erase() in v2. The author also removed unnecessary code and improved error handling.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the swapcached state being managed at the virtual swap layer by abstracting away the function in userfaultfd.c to check if a swap entry is cached, and provided patches to implement this abstraction.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing a concern about the patch series being incomplete, specifically that it doesn't include any behavioral changes yet. The author explains that they are only adding scaffolding for future code and that no behavioral change is intended at this time.
review_comments.raw_body:
In prepration for the implementation of swap virtualization, add new
scaffolds for the new code: a new mm/vswap.c source file, which
currently only holds the logic to set up the (for now, empty) vswap
debugfs directory. Hook this up in the swap setup step in
mm/swap_state.c, and set up vswap compilation in the Makefile.

Other than the debugfs directory, no behavioral change intended.

Finally, make Johannes a swap reviewer, given that he has contributed
majorly to the developments of virtual swap.

Signed-off-by: Nhat Pham <nphamcs@gmail.com>
---
 MAINTAINERS          |  2 ++
 include/linux/swap.h |  3 +++
 mm/Makefile          |  2 +-
 mm/swap_state.c      |  6 ++++++
 mm/vswap.c           | 35 +++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++
 5 files changed, 47 insertions(+), 1 deletion(-)
 create mode 100644 mm/vswap.c

diff --git a/MAINTAINERS b/MAINTAINERS
index d3780bb330378..042dbc06c3d3c 100644
--- a/MAINTAINERS
+++ b/MAINTAINERS
@@ -16728,6 +16728,7 @@ R:	Kemeng Shi <shikemeng@huaweicloud.com>
 R:	Nhat Pham <nphamcs@gmail.com>
 R:	Baoquan He <bhe@redhat.com>
 R:	Barry Song <baohua@kernel.org>
+R:	Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>
 L:	linux-mm@kvack.org
 S:	Maintained
 F:	include/linux/swap.h
@@ -16739,6 +16740,7 @@ F:	mm/swap.h
 F:	mm/swap_table.h
 F:	mm/swap_state.c
 F:	mm/swapfile.c
+F:	mm/vswap.c
 
 MEMORY MANAGEMENT - THP (TRANSPARENT HUGE PAGE)
 M:	Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org>
diff --git a/include/linux/swap.h b/include/linux/swap.h
index 918b47da55f44..df0771903a952 100644
--- a/include/linux/swap.h
+++ b/include/linux/swap.h
@@ -423,6 +423,9 @@ extern void __meminit kswapd_stop(int nid);
 
 #ifdef CONFIG_SWAP
 
+/* Virtual swap space API (mm/vswap.c) */
+int vswap_init(void);
+
 /* Lifecycle swap API (mm/swapfile.c) */
 int folio_alloc_swap(struct folio *folio);
 bool folio_free_swap(struct folio *folio);
diff --git a/mm/Makefile b/mm/Makefile
index 2d0570a16e5be..67fa4586e7e18 100644
--- a/mm/Makefile
+++ b/mm/Makefile
@@ -75,7 +75,7 @@ ifdef CONFIG_MMU
 	obj-$(CONFIG_ADVISE_SYSCALLS)	+= madvise.o
 endif
 
-obj-$(CONFIG_SWAP)	+= page_io.o swap_state.o swapfile.o
+obj-$(CONFIG_SWAP)	+= page_io.o swap_state.o swapfile.o vswap.o
 obj-$(CONFIG_ZSWAP)	+= zswap.o
 obj-$(CONFIG_HAS_DMA)	+= dmapool.o
 obj-$(CONFIG_HUGETLBFS)	+= hugetlb.o hugetlb_sysfs.o hugetlb_sysctl.o
diff --git a/mm/swap_state.c b/mm/swap_state.c
index e2e9f55bea3bb..29ec666be4204 100644
--- a/mm/swap_state.c
+++ b/mm/swap_state.c
@@ -882,6 +882,12 @@ static int __init swap_init(void)
 	int err;
 	struct kobject *swap_kobj;
 
+	err = vswap_init();
+	if (err) {
+		pr_err("failed to initialize virtual swap space\n");
+		return err;
+	}
+
 	swap_kobj = kobject_create_and_add("swap", mm_kobj);
 	if (!swap_kobj) {
 		pr_err("failed to create swap kobject\n");
diff --git a/mm/vswap.c b/mm/vswap.c
new file mode 100644
index 0000000000000..e68234f053fc9
--- /dev/null
+++ b/mm/vswap.c
@@ -0,0 +1,35 @@
+// SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-2.0
+/*
+ * Virtual swap space
+ *
+ * Copyright (C) 2024 Meta Platforms, Inc., Nhat Pham
+ */
+#include <linux/swap.h>
+
+#ifdef CONFIG_DEBUG_FS
+#include <linux/debugfs.h>
+
+static struct dentry *vswap_debugfs_root;
+
+static int vswap_debug_fs_init(void)
+{
+	if (!debugfs_initialized())
+		return -ENODEV;
+
+	vswap_debugfs_root = debugfs_create_dir("vswap", NULL);
+	return 0;
+}
+#else
+static int vswap_debug_fs_init(void)
+{
+	return 0;
+}
+#endif
+
+int vswap_init(void)
+{
+	if (vswap_debug_fs_init())
+		pr_warn("Failed to initialize vswap debugfs\n");
+
+	return 0;
+}
-- 
2.52.0

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about the separation of logical and physical swap views by introducing new types (swp_slot_t) to represent physical swap slots, which will be used in later patches for virtual swap allocation.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham (author)
review_comments.summary: The author acknowledged that the zswap tree code can no longer be reused for the new virtual swap space design and decided to use a simple unified zswap tree in the new implementation.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the freeing ordering of virtual swap slots, explaining that they are cleared and invalidated before releasing them, and acknowledged that this change is tied to the lifetime of their associated physical swap slot.

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: reviewer reported that the AI review tool was unable to apply the patches, and provided a link to a partial result
review_comments.raw_body:
AI review got partway through then decided it couldn't apply patches.  So
a partial result: https://sashiko.dev/#/patchset/20260320192735.748051-1-nphamcs@gmail.com

review_comments.author: Roman Gushchin
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Roman Gushchin noted that there was a bug in the error handling of the virtual swap space patch series, which he had already fixed but not deployed yet.
review_comments.raw_body:
It's a bug in the error handling. I've already fixed it, but haven't
deployed the new version yet. In the reality, the review failed for some
other reason (the most popular one now is backend/llm api transient errors).

Sashiko applies the entire patchset first and if it fails, it's not
reviewing anything.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 01/21] mm/swap: decouple swap cache from physical swap infrastructure
series_items.patch_summary: This patch decouples the swap cache from physical swap infrastructure, preparing for virtualization of swap space. It removes swap cache related helpers from the swap table and moves to a single global lock for synchronization. The swap cache will be re-partitioned into virtual swap clusters once it is moved to the virtual swap layer. This change is temporary and will be repurposed later as an rmap (physical -> virtual swap mapping).

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 02/21] swap: rearrange the swap header file
series_items.patch_summary: This patch rearranges the swap header file (include/linux/swap.h) to group related functions into categories such as lifecycle swap, swap cache, and physical swap allocator/swap device APIs. The goal is a cleaner organization of the code without introducing any functional changes. Extern keywords are removed from the functions that were rearranged.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 03/21] mm: swap: add an abstract API for locking out swapoff
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces an abstract API to lock out swapoff in the Linux kernel's memory management subsystem. The current implementation of locking out swapoff is specific to the backing swap device and does not work well with the new virtual swap design, where a swap entry may not have a backing swap device. To address this issue, the patch adds two new functions: `tryget_swap_entry` and `put_swap_entry`, which provide a generic way to lock out swapoff for any swap entry. The patch also updates several kernel files to use these new functions instead of the old implementation. This change prepares the kernel for the virtual swap design and improves its flexibility in handling different types of swap entries.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 04/21] zswap: add new helpers for zswap entry operations
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds new helper functions to abstract away zswap entry operations, making it easier to re-implement these functions when swap is virtualized. The helpers include storing, loading, erasing, and checking if a zswap entry is empty. These functions use the xarray data structure to manage the zswap entries. The patch modifies several existing functions in the mm/zswap.c file to use these new helpers, reducing code duplication and improving maintainability.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 05/21] mm/swap: add a new function to check if a swap entry is in swap cached.
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new function called `is_swap_cached` to the Linux kernel's memory management (mm) module. The function checks if a given swap entry is cached in the system's swap cache. This is done by directly accessing the swap map of the corresponding swap information structure, which minimizes overhead and ensures accurate results. The patch also updates several other functions to use this new function instead of directly checking the swap map. The motivation behind this change is to abstract away the check for swap cached state, as it will soon be managed at the virtual swap layer.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 06/21] mm: swap: add a separate type for physical swap slots
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new type, swp_slot_t, to represent physical swap slots on swap devices. It separates the logical view of swap entries from their physical backing state, allowing for future virtualization and dynamic allocation of swap slots. The mapping between the two types is currently an identity mapping, but will be used in later patches to associate swapped-out pages with a backing store.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 07/21] mm: create scaffolds for the new virtual swap implementation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds scaffolding code for the new virtual swap implementation, including a new mm/vswap.c source file and necessary changes to the Makefile and other related files. The vswap debugfs directory is set up, but no behavioral changes are intended at this time. Additionally, Johannes Weiner is added as a reviewer for the virtual swap development.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 08/21] zswap: prepare zswap for swap virtualization
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prepares the zswap code for swap virtualization by removing the range partition logic and replacing it with a simple unified zswap tree. The changes include updating the zswap.h header file to remove unused functions, modifying the swapfile.c file to remove calls to these functions, and updating the zswap.c file to use the new unified zswap tree. This patch is part of a larger series that aims to virtualize swap space in Linux.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 09/21] mm: swap: allocate a virtual swap slot for each swapped out page
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new virtual swap slot design, where each swapped out page is dynamically allocated a virtual slot and associated metadata structure. The virtual slot replaces the physical swap slot in page table entries and is used to index into swap data structures. The patch also repurposes the swap table infrastructure as a reverse map to look up the virtual swap slot from its associated physical swap slot on the swapfile. This change improves the management of swapped out pages and enables future optimizations such as simplified swapoff.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 10/21] swap: move swap cache to virtual swap descriptor
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the swap cache and workingset shadow for anonymous pages to the virtual swap descriptor, effectively range-partitioning the swap cache by virtual swap clusters of PMD size. This change eliminates lock contention in the swap cache, improving performance. The patch updates several kernel files, including huge_memory.c, migrate.c, shmem.c, and vmscan.c, to use the new virtual swap descriptor instead of the global swap cache lock. The approach taken is to introduce a per-folio swap pointer that allows for more efficient locking and reduces contention in the swap cache.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 11/21] zswap: move zswap entry management to the virtual swap descriptor
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves zswap entry management from the zswap tree to the virtual swap descriptor, eliminating lock contention between zswap entries. The change re-partitions the zswap pool by virtual swap cluster and introduces new functions for storing, loading, and erasing zswap entries in the swap descriptor. This approach simplifies the management of zswap entries and improves performance by reducing lock contention.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 12/21] swap: implement the swap_cgroup API using virtual swap
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the swap_cgroup API to use virtual swap, allowing for dynamic allocation of memory for swap entries' cgroup information. This reduces memory overhead in large but sparsely used swap spaces by only allocating memory when needed. The change involves moving the swap entry's cgroup information from a static array to the swap descriptor and updating various functions that interact with the swap_cgroup API.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 13/21] swap: manage swap entry lifecycle at the virtual swap layer
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the management of swap entry lifecycle to the virtual swap layer, adding two fields to the swap descriptor: in_swapcache and swap count. It re-implements all swap entry lifecycle API functions in the virtual swap layer, simplifying the logic by not implementing swap count continuation for now.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 14/21] mm: swap: decouple virtual swap slot from backing store
series_items.patch_summary: This patch decouples the virtual swap slot from its backing store, allowing a swap entry to be backed by one of four options: physical swap slot, zero swap page, compressed object in zswap pool, or in-memory page. This change enables the use of zswap and zero swap page optimization without reserving a swapfile slot, resulting in significant disk savings for hosts with high memory usage. The patch also introduces new requirements for batched swap operations to ensure consistency across entries with different backing states.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 15/21] zswap: do not start zswap shrinker if there is no physical swap slots
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prevents the zswap shrinker from starting if there are no physical swap slots available, which is a consideration for virtualized swap environments where pre-allocated swap slots do not exist.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 16/21] swap: do not unnecesarily pin readahead swap entries
series_items.patch_summary: This patch optimizes swap readahead by not unnecessarily pinning entries in the same virtual swap cluster as the target entry, which is already pinned by the caller. The patch introduces a new function `vswap_same_cluster` to check if two virtual swap entries belong to the same vswap cluster. It then uses this function to skip unnecessary pinning of readahead entries that are already protected by the target entry's cluster. This change aims to improve performance and reduce overhead in swap operations.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 17/21] swapfile: remove zeromap bitmap
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes the zeromap bitmap from the swapfile code in the Linux kernel. The zeromap bitmap was used to track zero pages, but since zero swap entries are now treated as a separate backend in the virtual swap layer, this bitmap is no longer needed. Removing it saves memory overhead by eliminating 1 bit per swap page. This change does not affect behavior and only optimizes memory usage.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 18/21] memcg: swap: only charge physical swap slots
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's memory cgroup (memcg) swap handling to only charge physical swap slots, rather than virtual swap slots. This change is made possible by the removal of zswap and zero-filled swap page optimization from consuming physical swap space. The patch updates various functions in the memcontrol code to record and clear swap charges for memcgs, ensuring that only actual physical swap usage is accounted for.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 19/21] swap: simplify swapoff using virtual swap
series_items.patch_summary: This patch simplifies and optimizes the swapoff process by utilizing virtual swap slots stored at page table entries, eliminating the need for a page table walk. Instead of traversing all VMAs, it iterates through allocated swap slots on the swapfile, finds their corresponding virtual swap slots, and faults them in. This results in cleaner code and a performance improvement of about 13% kernel CPU time reduction compared to the original baseline.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 20/21] swapfile: replace the swap map with bitmaps
series_items.patch_summary: This patch replaces the swap map in the Linux kernel's swapfile code with two bitmaps, one for tracking allocated swap entries and another for bad swap entries. This change is made possible by moving the swap count state to the virtual swap layer, which reduces the number of states that need to be tracked from 4 to 3. The use of bitmaps instead of a single array saves 6 bits per swap entry, reducing memory usage. The patch updates the swap_info_struct structure in include/linux/swap.h and modifies several functions in mm/swapfile.c to work with the new bitmap-based representation.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch optimizes the vswap_free function by batching contiguous free calls to reduce overhead on non-disk-swap backends. It achieves this by introducing a new release_vswap_slot_nr function that takes an additional 'nr' parameter, which specifies the number of slots to be released. This allows for more efficient handling of consecutive free operations and reduces unnecessary lock drops.
